flashcards.front: What is the difference between a program and a process?
flashcards.back: A program is a passive file stored on disk, while a process is an active program in execution with its own execution context, memory, and resources.

flashcards.front: What are the five fundamental process states in an OS?
flashcards.back: New (created), Ready (waiting in queue for CPU), Running (currently executing), Waiting (blocked for I/O/event), Terminated (finished execution).

flashcards.front: What is a Process Control Block (PCB)?
flashcards.back: PCB is a data structure maintained by the kernel that stores all information about a process, including registers, program counter, memory management info, I/O status, and accounting data.

flashcards.front: What information is stored in a PCB?
flashcards.back: Process ID, Process State, Program Counter, CPU Registers, Memory Limits, List of Open Files, Scheduling Information, Accounting Information, I/O Status.
Context switching is the process of saving and restoring the state (CPU registers, program counter) of a process so that execution can be resumed from the same point later.

flashcards.front: What is the role of the Dispatcher in process scheduling?
flashcards.back: The Dispatcher is responsible for moving a process from Ready state to Running state, performing context switch, and giving control of CPU to the selected process.
A zombie process is a terminated process whose parent has not yet read its exit status. It remains in the process table until the parent collects its termination status.
An orphan process is a running process whose parent has terminated. In Unix, orphan processes are adopted by init (PID 1) which waits for their termination.
Process scheduling is the method by which the OS decides which ready process to allocate to the CPU at any given time, balancing throughput, turnaround time, and CPU utilization.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling?
flashcards.back: Preemptive allows higher-priority processes to interrupt currently running processes; non-preemptive lets a process run to completion or until it voluntarily yields CPU.

flashcards.front: What is a suspended process state?
flashcards.back: Suspended state occurs when a process is swapped out of main memory to secondary storage. The process is not ready nor running - it is completely out of memory.
A daemon is a background-running process that starts at system boot and runs continuously, providing system services (like printing, networking) without user interaction.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the process table?
flashcards.back: The process table is an array of PCBs maintained by the OS that maintains information about all currently active processes in the system.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between threads and processes?
flashcards.back: A process can contain one or more threads. Threads share process resources (memory, files) but have their own registers, program counter, and stack.

flashcards.front: What causes a process to transition from Running to Ready state?
flashcards.back: A running process transitions to Ready state due to: preemption by scheduler, time slice expiration (in round-robin), or voluntary yield of CPU.
